content:
Before dedicated **experiment trackers**, practitioners logged metrics to **text files**, **spreadsheets**, and **stdout**— one number per run with little structure.

**Neural network** research in the 1980s–1990s popularized plotting **training error vs validation error** over epochs on paper and in TeX figures— the scalar curve became the standard diagnostic for overfitting.

**TensorBoard** (2015, TensorFlow ecosystem) generalized **scalar summaries** with named tags and step axes— teams could watch train/loss and val/loss live during long GPU jobs.

**MLflow** (2018) and **Weights & Biases** (2018) made **metric logging APIs** and **cross-run comparison** first-class— metric keys, steps, and run metadata became part of reproducible experiment records.

Modern **MLOps** treats metric naming conventions as **team standards**: split prefixes, primary selection metrics, and one-shot test logging are documented beside hyperparameter grids. The idea unchanged from classical hold-out evaluation: **record the right scores on the right splits over time so development decisions are traceable.**

timeline.label: Manual logs (pre-2000s)
timeline.description: Researchers record train and validation error by hand or script to text— few conventions, hard to compare many runs.

timeline.label: Validation curves standard (1990s)
timeline.description: ML textbooks emphasize plotting training vs validation error over epochs as the primary overfitting diagnostic.

timeline.label: TensorBoard scalars (2015)
timeline.description: Named scalar tags and step axes enable live train/val curves during deep learning training.

timeline.label: Experiment trackers (2018+)
timeline.description: MLflow and W&B codify run-level metric logging, hyperparameter association, and cross-run curve comparison.

timeline.label: Naming standards in MLOps (2020s)
timeline.description: Teams document split-prefixed metric keys and primary validation metrics as part of experiment contracts and release checklists.
